title: Essential Dimension via Prismatic Cohomology
speaker: Mark Kisin
abstract: Let f:Y→X be a finite covering map of complex algebraic varieties. The essential dimension of f is the smallest integer e such that, birationally, f arises as the pullback of a covering Y′→X′ of dimension e, via a map X→X′. This invariant goes back to classical questions about reducing the number of parameters in a solution to a general nth degree polynomial, and appeared in work of Kronecker and Klein on solutions of the quintic.

proof_stages.label: Essential dimension and p-essential dimension of covers
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines when a finite cover of a variety is obtained, after restricting to a dense open, by pullback from a lower-dimensional cover, and introduces the p-essential variant and incompressibility.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic incompressibility targets
proof_stages.summary: The principal results are formulated: multiplication-by-p covers of abelian varieties, more general mod-p homology covers, and principal p-congruence covers of locally symmetric/Shimura varieties are p-incompressible under good-reduction and size hypotheses.

proof_stages.label: Arnold cohomological lower-bound criterion
proof_stages.summary: For a G-cover, the associated map from H*(G,F_p) to the cohomology of dense opens of X gives lower bounds on essential dimension whenever a degree-i characteristic class survives at the generic point.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to a characteristic-class calculation and a restriction theorem
proof_stages.summary: The lower-bound criterion is reduced to two inputs: a case-by-case surjectivity calculation for H^i(G,F_p) to H^i(X,F_p), and a general theorem ensuring that restriction from X to opens does not kill enough mod-p cohomology.

proof_stages.label: General mod-p restriction theorem
proof_stages.summary: For a smooth proper variety with good reduction at p and p at least i+2, the image of H^i_et(X,F_p) in the cohomology of any dense/open or formal affine open has dimension at least h^{0,i}, matching the Hodge-theoretic expectation.

proof_stages.label: Prismatic comparison package
proof_stages.summary: The proof uses mod-p prismatic cohomology over k[[u]], together with its comparison after inverting u to étale cohomology, its specialization at u=0 to mod-p de Rham cohomology, and its Frobenius isogeny property.

proof_stages.label: Torsion-freeness of the prismatic image
proof_stages.summary: A key lemma shows that the image of prismatic cohomology under restriction has no u-torsion when p is at least i+2, using a minimal torsion exponent and the Frobenius isogeny to force a contradictory inequality.

proof_stages.label: Contradiction from de Rham specialization and conclusion
proof_stages.summary: If the étale restriction image were zero, the prismatic image would be u-torsion and hence zero by the lemma, but specialization to de Rham cohomology would then kill the nonzero contribution from global i-forms, giving the required contradiction.

examples.name: Generic polynomial and the Tschirnhaus-type coefficient shift
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker describes the cover of the coefficient space of a general degree-m polynomial and the classical change of variables setting one coefficient to zero as an obvious compression mechanism.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker concretely describes the generic degree-m polynomial over coefficient space and applies the classical coefficient-shift change of variables to set the sum coefficient to zero and obtain a compression.

passes.reason: The generic polynomial cover is a standard family whose Tschirnhaus shift compresses it, showing naive incompressibility can fail.

examples.name: Q-coefficient Hodge-theoretic analogue

examples.engagement_reason: The Q-coefficient analogue is explained via Hodge structures, tensoring with C, the contribution of global i-forms, and the fact that restricting a holomorphic form remains nonzero.
